1:
"This know also, that in the last days perilous times shall come." The brief verse notes the warning of perilous times in the last days. The grammatical voices note the prediction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's solemn forewarning: "this know also, that in the last days (the latter times of the present age, stretching to Christ's return) perilous (chalepoi — dangerous, hard, grievous, fierce) times shall come" — Timothy is to know that seasons of moral danger and difficulty will mark the age.

On "the last days," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes and Calvin note that "the last days" embraces the whole gospel age (the days of the Messiah), with special reference to periods of declension within it; the perils Paul describes were already beginning and would recur and intensify.

On "perilous times," the eastern Chrysostom notes the danger: the times are "perilous" not chiefly from persecution but from the corruption of morals and religion he proceeds to describe (vv.2-5); the danger is to faith and holiness. The verse displays the warning of perilous times in the last days.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's forewarning — "in the last days (the latter age) perilous (dangerous, grievous) times shall come," seasons of moral danger marking the age (Barnes, Robertson); "the last days" embracing the whole gospel age with special reference to declensions, already beginning (Barnes, Calvin); "perilous" from corruption of morals and religion, not chiefly persecution (Chrysostom). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the knowledge: this know. The verse displays the time: the last days. The verse displays the peril: dangerous times. The verse displays the danger: to faith and morals. The warning displays Paul forewarning of dangerous times. The verse displays the days: last. The verse displays the times: perilous. The verse displays the coming: foretold. The warning of perilous times in the last days displays the solemn forewarning that opens the chapter: Timothy is to know that the latter age will bring dangerous, grievous times — perilous not so much from persecution as from the corruption of morals and religion now to be described. The verse displays the perilous times, detailed in a catalogue of vices (vv.2-5).
2:
"For men shall be lovers of their own selves, covetous, boasters, proud, blasphemers, disobedient to parents, unthankful, unholy," The brief verse notes the catalogue of vices marking the perilous times, beginning with self-love. The grammatical voices note the root sin. Barnes and Robertson note that the list of corruptions opens with the root: "men shall be lovers of their own selves (philautoi — self-lovers, selfish), covetous (lovers of money)" — selfishness and greed head the catalogue, the springs of the rest.

On "boasters, proud, blasphemers," the witnesses note the sins of pride and speech. Barnes and Calvin note the next cluster: "boasters (braggarts), proud (haughty, arrogant), blasphemers (railers, evil-speakers against God or men)" — the swelling of pride and the abuse of the tongue.

On "disobedient to parents, unthankful, unholy," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the breakdown of natural duty: "disobedient to parents (rebellious), unthankful (ungrateful), unholy (impure, profane, without reverence)" — the collapse of the most basic bonds of duty, gratitude, and reverence. The verse displays the catalogue of vices beginning with self-love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the list opens with the root — "lovers of their own selves (selfish), covetous (lovers of money)," selfishness and greed the springs of the rest (Barnes, Robertson); "boasters, proud, blasphemers," the swelling of pride and abuse of the tongue (Barnes, Calvin); "disobedient to parents, unthankful, unholy," the collapse of natural duty, gratitude, and reverence (Wesley). The verse displays the vices. The verse marks the root: lovers of self. The verse displays the greed: covetous. The verse displays the pride: boasters, proud. The verse displays the breakdown: disobedient, unthankful, unholy. The catalogue displays the corruptions springing from self-love. The verse displays the self-love: first. The verse displays the pride: swelling. The verse displays the duty: broken. The catalogue of vices marking the perilous times, beginning with self-love, displays the moral corruption Paul foretells: men will be lovers of themselves and of money, boastful, proud, blasphemous, disobedient to parents, ungrateful, and profane — the basic bonds of duty and reverence dissolving. The verse displays the corruption of the last days, continued in further vices (v.3).
3:
"Without natural affection, trucebreakers, false accusers, incontinent, fierce, despisers of those that are good," The brief verse notes the catalogue of vices continued: the loss of affection and self-control. The grammatical voices note the unnatural cruelty. Barnes and Robertson note the next cluster: "without natural affection (astorgoi — devoid of family love, hard-hearted toward kin), trucebreakers (aspondoi — implacable, covenant-breaking, irreconcilable), false accusers (diaboloi — slanderers, devils)" — the absence of natural love and the malice of slander.

On "incontinent, fierce," the witnesses note the loss of restraint. Barnes and Calvin note: "incontinent (akrateis — without self-control, intemperate) and fierce (anemeroi — savage, brutal, untamed)" — the loss of all self-mastery and the rise of brutal savagery.

On "despisers of those that are good," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the hatred of good: "despisers of those that are good (aphilagathoi — haters of good and of good men)" — not only doing evil but hating goodness itself and those who practise it; enmity against the good. The verse displays the catalogue continued: the loss of affection and self-control.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the cluster — "without natural affection (devoid of family love), trucebreakers (implacable), false accusers (slanderers)," the absence of love and malice of slander (Barnes, Robertson); "incontinent (without self-control), fierce (savage, brutal)," the loss of self-mastery and rise of brutality (Barnes, Calvin); "despisers of those that are good (haters of good)," hating goodness itself (Wesley). The verse displays the vices. The verse marks the unnatural: without affection. The verse displays the malice: false accusers. The verse displays the loss: incontinent, fierce. The verse displays the enmity: despisers of good. The catalogue displays affection and restraint dissolving into savagery. The verse displays the affection: lost. The verse displays the self-control: gone. The verse displays the good: hated. The catalogue of vices continued — the loss of affection and self-control — displays the deepening corruption: men will be without natural affection, implacable, slanderous, without self-control, savage, and haters of the good — natural love and restraint giving way to brutality and enmity against goodness. The verse displays the savagery of the last days, completed by treachery and pleasure-love (v.4).
4:
"Traitors, heady, highminded, lovers of pleasures more than lovers of God;" The brief verse notes the catalogue's climax: pleasure-lovers rather than God-lovers. The grammatical voices note the treachery and rashness. Barnes and Robertson note the next vices: "traitors (prodotai — betrayers, treacherous), heady (propeteis — rash, reckless, headstrong), highminded (tetyphomenoi — puffed up, conceited, beclouded with pride)" — treachery, recklessness, and swollen conceit.

On "lovers of pleasures more than lovers of God," the witnesses note the root contrast. Barnes and Calvin note the climactic vice: "lovers of pleasures (philedonoi) more than lovers of God (philotheoi)" — the great antithesis; pleasure dethrones God in the heart; men love their pleasures above their Maker, the deepest mark of the corruption.

On the wordplay, the grammatical-historical note the pointed contrast: the catalogue that began with "lovers of self" (v.2) ends with "lovers of pleasure more than lovers of God" — self-love and pleasure-love framing the whole, with God displaced throughout. The verse displays the catalogue's climax: pleasure-lovers rather than God-lovers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vices — "traitors (betrayers), heady (rash, reckless), highminded (puffed up, conceited)," treachery, recklessness, and conceit (Barnes, Robertson); the climax "lovers of pleasures more than lovers of God," pleasure dethroning God in the heart, the deepest mark of corruption (Barnes, Calvin); the catalogue framed by self-love and pleasure-love, God displaced throughout (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the climax. The verse marks the treachery: traitors. The verse displays the rashness: heady. The verse displays the conceit: highminded. The verse displays the climax: pleasure above God. The catalogue displays pleasure dethroning God in the heart. The verse displays the traitors: treacherous. The verse displays the pleasure: loved. The verse displays the God: displaced. The catalogue's climax — pleasure-lovers rather than God-lovers — displays the deepest root of the corruption: men will be treacherous, reckless, and conceited, and above all lovers of pleasure more than lovers of God — pleasure usurping the throne God should hold in the heart. The verse displays the displacing of God, masked by a form of godliness (v.5).
5:
"Having a form of godliness, but denying the power thereof: from such turn away." The witnesses note the form of godliness without its power, and the charge to turn away. On "having a form of godliness, but denying the power thereof," the grammatical voices note the hypocrisy. Barnes and Robertson note the crowning trait of these corrupt men: "having a form (morphosis — outward shape, appearance, semblance) of godliness, but denying (renouncing, repudiating in practice) the power thereof" — they keep the outward show of religion while denying its inward, transforming power; a hollow, hypocritical religion.

On "denying the power thereof," the witnesses note the emptiness. Barnes and Calvin note that the "power of godliness" is its real, inward efficacy — the grace that renews and sanctifies; these men retain the external forms (profession, worship, ceremony) but their lives deny that religion has any real power over them.

On "from such turn away," the puritan Henry notes the charge: "from such turn away (avoid, shun, keep clear of)" — Timothy is to separate himself from such hypocrites; not to associate with those whose religion is mere form. The verse displays the form of godliness without its power.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the crowning trait — "having a form (outward semblance) of godliness, but denying (repudiating in practice) the power thereof," keeping the show while denying the inward power, a hypocritical religion (Barnes, Robertson); the "power" the real inward efficacy of grace, their lives denying religion has any power over them (Barnes, Calvin); "from such turn away (shun, avoid)," separating from the hypocrites (Henry). The verse displays the empty form. The verse marks the show: a form of godliness. The verse displays the denial: the power. The verse displays the hypocrisy: outward only. The verse displays the charge: turn away. The form displays religion's shell without its substance. The verse displays the form: outward. The verse displays the power: denied. The verse displays the turning: away. The form of godliness without its power, and the charge to turn away, display the religious hypocrisy that crowns the catalogue: these corrupt men keep the outward shape of godliness while denying its inward, transforming power — and from such empty professors Timothy is to turn away. The verse displays the hollow religion, illustrated by deceivers who prey on the weak (v.6).
6:
"For of this sort are they which creep into houses, and lead captive silly women laden with sins, led away with divers lusts," The brief verse notes the deceivers who creep into houses and lead captive weak women. The grammatical voices note the insidious method. Barnes and Robertson note the description of these false teachers' tactics: "of this sort are they which creep into (worm their way into, insinuate themselves into) houses (households), and lead captive (take captive, enslave) silly women (gynaikaria — weak, foolish little women)" — they gain entrance by stealth and prey on the spiritually weak.

On "silly women laden with sins," the witnesses note the victims. Barnes and Calvin note the description: "silly women laden with sins (heaped with guilt, burdened with an evil conscience)" — women weighed down by sin and a troubled conscience, easy prey for those who offer false comfort or novelty; the burdened and unstable are the deceivers' target.

On "led away with divers lusts," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the inner bondage: such women are "led away (driven, controlled) with divers (various) lusts" — ruled by changing desires and passions, making them susceptible to seduction; their own lusts deliver them into the deceivers' hands. The verse displays the deceivers who creep into houses and lead captive weak women.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the tactics — "they which creep into (worm into) houses, and lead captive (enslave) silly women (weak, foolish)," gaining entrance by stealth and preying on the weak (Barnes, Robertson); "laden with sins (heaped with guilt)," women burdened by sin and conscience, easy prey (Barnes, Calvin); "led away with divers lusts," ruled by changing passions, susceptible to seduction (Wesley). The verse displays the deceivers. The verse marks the method: creep into houses. The verse displays the prey: silly women. The verse displays the burden: laden with sins. The verse displays the bondage: divers lusts. The deceivers display false teachers preying on the weak and burdened. The verse displays the creeping: stealthy. The verse displays the captives: weak women. The verse displays the lusts: ruling. The deceivers who creep into houses and lead captive weak women display the insidious method of the hypocrites: they worm their way into households and enslave foolish women — burdened with sins and driven by various lusts — preying on the spiritually weak and unstable. The verse displays the deceivers' prey, ever learning but never knowing truth (v.7).
7:
"Ever learning, and never able to come to the knowledge of the truth." The brief verse notes the deceived women ever learning but never knowing the truth. The grammatical voices note the fruitless study. Barnes and Robertson note the pitiable state of these captive women: "ever learning (always being taught, forever taking up new instruction), and never able to come to the knowledge (full knowledge, epignosis) of the truth" — perpetually занятые with novelty, yet never arriving at real, saving truth.

On "ever learning," the witnesses note the restless novelty. Barnes and Calvin note that such persons are always seeking, always listening to new teachers and ideas, but with no settled grasp; their learning is endless motion without arrival, curiosity without conviction.

On "never able to come to the knowledge of the truth," the grammatical-historical note the cause: they are "never able" — not from lack of opportunity but from their own unstable, lust-ruled condition (v.6); the heart enslaved to sin cannot reach the truth, however much it hears. The verse displays the deceived women ever learning but never knowing the truth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the pitiable state — "ever learning (always being taught), and never able to come to the knowledge (full knowledge) of the truth," perpetually busy with novelty yet never arriving at saving truth (Barnes, Robertson); always seeking new teachers but with no settled grasp, curiosity without conviction (Barnes, Calvin); "never able" not from lack of opportunity but from their unstable, lust-ruled condition (grammatical-historical). The verse displays fruitless learning. The verse marks the learning: ever. The verse displays the failure: never knowing truth. The verse displays the novelty: restless. The verse displays the cause: the enslaved heart. The state displays endless study without arrival at truth. The verse displays the learning: perpetual. The verse displays the knowledge: never reached. The verse displays the truth: missed. The deceived women ever learning but never knowing the truth display the fruitless end of their captivity: forever taking up new instruction, they never arrive at the real, saving knowledge of the truth — for the heart enslaved to sin and shifting lusts cannot reach it. The verse displays the failure of mere novelty, like the old resistance of Jannes and Jambres (v.8).
8:
"Now as Jannes and Jambres withstood Moses, so do these also resist the truth: men of corrupt minds, reprobate concerning the faith." The witnesses note the deceivers resisting the truth like Jannes and Jambres. On "as Jannes and Jambres withstood Moses," the grammatical voices note the example. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison drawn from tradition: "as Jannes and Jambres (the names, from Jewish tradition, of the Egyptian magicians who opposed Moses before Pharaoh, Exodus 7) withstood (resisted, opposed) Moses" — the magicians who imitated Moses's signs to oppose God's truth.

On "so do these also resist the truth," the witnesses note the parallel. Barnes and Calvin note the application: "so do these also resist (oppose, set themselves against) the truth" — as the magicians counterfeited and opposed Moses, so the false teachers counterfeit and resist the gospel; opposition to the truth by imitation and deceit.

On "men of corrupt minds, reprobate concerning the faith," the grammatical-historical note their character: they are "men of corrupt (depraved, debased) minds, reprobate (adokimoi — rejected, failing the test, counterfeit) concerning the faith" — their understanding is corrupted and, tested by the faith, they are found counterfeit and rejected. The verse displays the deceivers resisting the truth like Jannes and Jambres.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comparison — "as Jannes and Jambres (the Egyptian magicians of tradition, Exodus 7) withstood Moses," imitating his signs to oppose God's truth (Barnes, Robertson); "so do these also resist the truth," counterfeiting and opposing the gospel as the magicians did Moses (Barnes, Calvin); "men of corrupt minds, reprobate (rejected, counterfeit) concerning the faith," their understanding depraved, found counterfeit when tested (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the resisters. The verse marks the example: Jannes and Jambres. The verse displays the parallel: resist the truth. The verse displays the corruption: of minds. The verse displays the verdict: reprobate. The deceivers display false teachers opposing truth by counterfeit. The verse displays the magicians: withstood Moses. The verse displays the resisting: of truth. The verse displays the reprobate: rejected. The deceivers resisting the truth like Jannes and Jambres display the character and doom of the false teachers: as the Egyptian magicians counterfeited Moses's signs to oppose him, so these resist the gospel — men of corrupt minds, counterfeit and rejected when tested by the faith. The verse displays the resisters of truth, whose folly shall be exposed (v.9).
9:
"But they shall proceed no further: for their folly shall be manifest unto all men, as theirs also was." The brief verse notes that the deceivers' folly will be exposed and their progress checked. The grammatical voices note the limit. Barnes and Robertson note the comfort: "but they shall proceed no further (advance no more, be stopped)" — the false teachers' success has a limit; God will check their progress, they will not prevail in the end.

On "their folly shall be manifest unto all men," the witnesses note the exposure. Barnes and Calvin note the means of their check: "their folly (madness, senselessness) shall be manifest (made plain, exposed) unto all men" — their deceit and error will be openly exposed, their pretensions unmasked before all; truth will out.

On "as theirs also was," the grammatical-historical note the precedent: "as theirs (Jannes and Jambres) also was" — as the Egyptian magicians were finally exposed and confounded, unable to match Moses's later signs (Exodus 8:18-19), so these deceivers will be unmasked; the precedent guarantees the outcome. The verse displays the deceivers' folly exposed and their progress checked.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comfort — "they shall proceed no further (be stopped)," their success limited, God checking their progress (Barnes, Robertson); "their folly (madness) shall be manifest (exposed) unto all men," their deceit openly unmasked, truth winning out (Barnes, Calvin); "as theirs (the magicians) also was," exposed and confounded as Jannes and Jambres were (Exodus 8:18-19), the precedent guaranteeing it (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the check. The verse marks the limit: proceed no further. The verse displays the exposure: folly manifest. The verse displays the witness: all men. The verse displays the precedent: as theirs. The check displays the deceivers stopped and exposed. The verse displays the proceeding: halted. The verse displays the folly: manifest. The verse displays the precedent: the magicians. That the deceivers' folly will be exposed and their progress checked displays the comfort closing the warning: the false teachers will not advance indefinitely, for their madness will be made plain to all — just as the folly of Jannes and Jambres was finally exposed before Pharaoh. The verse displays the limit of error, over against Timothy's faithful following of Paul (vv.10-11).
10:
"But thou hast fully known my doctrine, manner of life, purpose, faith, longsuffering, charity, patience," The witnesses note Timothy's full knowledge of Paul's doctrine and life. On "thou hast fully known my doctrine, manner of life," the grammatical voices note the contrast and the intimacy. Barnes and Robertson note the turn from the deceivers to Timothy: "but thou hast fully known (parekolouthesas — closely followed, traced out, been a close witness to) my doctrine (teaching), manner of life (conduct, way of living)" — over against the false teachers, Timothy had intimately known both Paul's teaching and his life.

On "purpose, faith, longsuffering, charity, patience," the witnesses note the catalogue of graces. Barnes and Henry note the list, answering the catalogue of vices (vv.2-4): "purpose (his aim, resolve), faith, longsuffering (patient endurance of wrong), charity (love), patience (steadfast endurance)" — the graces that marked Paul's whole life, a pattern over against the corruption described.

On the whole, the grammatical-historical note the witness: Timothy had been a close, long-term companion who had observed not only what Paul taught but how he lived, suffered, and persevered; doctrine and life together. The verse displays Timothy's full knowledge of Paul's doctrine and life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the turn — "thou hast fully known (closely followed) my doctrine (teaching), manner of life (conduct)," Timothy intimately knowing Paul's teaching and life over against the false teachers (Barnes, Robertson); the catalogue of graces "purpose, faith, longsuffering, charity (love), patience," answering the catalogue of vices, the pattern of Paul's life (Barnes, Henry); Timothy a close witness of both doctrine and life (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the known pattern. The verse marks the knowledge: fully known. The verse displays the doctrine: Paul's teaching. The verse displays the life: his conduct. The verse displays the graces: faith, love, patience. The knowledge displays Timothy following Paul's teaching and life. The verse displays the following: close. The verse displays the doctrine: known. The verse displays the graces: observed. Timothy's full knowledge of Paul's doctrine and life displays the bright contrast with the deceivers: Timothy had closely followed and intimately known not only Paul's teaching but his whole manner of life — his purpose, faith, longsuffering, love, and patience — a living pattern over against the corruption of the age. The verse displays the known example, including Paul's persecutions (v.11).
11:
"Persecutions, afflictions, which came unto me at Antioch, at Iconium, at Lystra; what persecutions I endured: but out of them all the Lord delivered me." The witnesses note the persecutions Paul endured and the Lord's deliverance. On "persecutions, afflictions, which came unto me at Antioch, at Iconium, at Lystra," the grammatical voices note the recollection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul recalls his sufferings, which Timothy had known: "persecutions, afflictions, which came unto me at Antioch (Pisidian), at Iconium, at Lystra" — the cities of Paul's first journey (Acts 13-14), where he was opposed, driven out, and at Lystra stoned; places near Timothy's home, well known to him.

On "what persecutions I endured," the witnesses note the endurance. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's emphasis on his steadfastness: "what persecutions I endured (bore, held out under)" — not merely that he suffered, but that he endured; his constancy under persecution was part of the example Timothy knew.

On "but out of them all the Lord delivered me," the puritan Henry notes the deliverance: "but out of them all the Lord delivered (rescued) me" — through every persecution the Lord brought him safely; the faithful sufferer is not abandoned but delivered, an encouragement to Timothy facing his own trials. The verse displays the persecutions Paul endured and the Lord's deliverance.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul recalls — "persecutions, afflictions... at Antioch, Iconium, Lystra," the cities of his first journey (Acts 13-14) where he was opposed and stoned, near Timothy's home (Barnes, Robertson); "what persecutions I endured (held out under)," his steadfast constancy part of the example (Barnes, Calvin); "but out of them all the Lord delivered (rescued) me," the Lord bringing him safely through every trial (Henry). The verse displays the deliverance. The verse marks the persecutions: Antioch, Iconium, Lystra. The verse displays the endurance: what I endured. The verse displays the deliverance: out of them all. The verse displays the deliverer: the Lord. The recollection displays Paul persecuted yet delivered by the Lord. The verse displays the afflictions: suffered. The verse displays the enduring: steadfast. The verse displays the Lord: delivering. The persecutions Paul endured and the Lord's deliverance display the example Timothy knew: Paul suffered persecution at Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra — enduring all steadfastly — yet out of them all the Lord delivered him; suffering and deliverance together encouraging Timothy. The verse displays the suffering and rescue, with the warning that the godly will suffer (v.12).
12:
"Yea, and all that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution." The brief verse notes the general truth that the godly will suffer persecution. The grammatical voices note the universal principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws a general law from his own case: "yea, and all that will live godly (eusebos — piously, devoutly) in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution (be persecuted)" — not Paul only, but all who are resolved to live a godly life in union with Christ will meet persecution; it is the common lot of the faithful.

On "all that will live godly," the witnesses note the resolve. Barnes and Calvin note the force of "will live godly" — those who are determined and resolved to live devoutly; the godly life itself, by its contrast with the world, provokes opposition; holiness draws persecution as light exposes darkness.

On "shall suffer persecution," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the certainty: persecution in some form is the appointed portion of the godly — not always violent, but always some measure of the world's hostility; the cross belongs to the Christian life. The verse displays the general truth that the godly will suffer persecution.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the general law — "all that will live godly (piously) in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution," not Paul only but all the resolved godly, the common lot of the faithful (Barnes, Robertson); "will live godly" meaning those determined to live devoutly, holiness provoking opposition (Barnes, Calvin); persecution in some form the appointed portion of the godly, the cross belonging to the Christian life (Wesley). The verse displays the law of persecution. The verse marks the scope: all the godly. The verse displays the life: godly in Christ. The verse displays the certainty: shall suffer persecution. The verse displays the cause: holiness provokes. The truth displays the godly meeting the world's hostility. The verse displays the godliness: resolved. The verse displays the persecution: certain. The verse displays the lot: common. The general truth that the godly will suffer persecution displays the law Paul draws from his own sufferings: all who are resolved to live a devout life in Christ Jesus will meet persecution — for holiness, by its very contrast with the world, draws the world's hostility. The verse displays the cross of the godly, against the worsening of the wicked (v.13).
13:
"But evil men and seducers shall wax worse and worse, deceiving, and being deceived." The brief verse notes that evil men and seducers will grow worse, deceiving and deceived. The grammatical voices note the downward course. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the persecuted godly: "but evil men and seducers (goetes — impostors, sorcerers, deceivers) shall wax worse and worse (proceed from bad to worse, advance in evil)" — the wicked, far from improving, will progress in corruption; evil is self-deepening.

On "deceiving, and being deceived," the witnesses note the double bondage. Barnes and Calvin note the twofold ruin: "deceiving (others), and being deceived (themselves)" — they both lead others astray and are themselves led astray; the deceiver is also a dupe, caught in his own delusion; error spreads and recoils.

On the progression, the grammatical-historical note the realism: this is not a promise of the world's improvement but a warning of deepening corruption among the wicked; the godly suffer (v.12) while the evil grow worse — the two courses diverging. The verse displays that evil men and seducers will grow worse, deceiving and deceived.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "evil men and seducers (impostors, deceivers) shall wax worse and worse (advance in evil)," the wicked progressing in corruption, evil self-deepening (Barnes, Robertson); "deceiving (others), and being deceived (themselves)," both leading astray and led astray, the deceiver his own dupe (Barnes, Calvin); a warning of deepening corruption, not the world's improvement (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worsening. The verse marks the men: evil and seducers. The verse displays the course: worse and worse. The verse displays the deceit: deceiving. The verse displays the bondage: being deceived. The progression displays the wicked deepening in delusion. The verse displays the evil: advancing. The verse displays the deceiving: of others. The verse displays the deceived: themselves. That evil men and seducers will grow worse, deceiving and deceived, displays the dark counterpart to the suffering of the godly: the wicked and the impostors do not improve but advance from bad to worse — both deceiving others and being deceived themselves, caught in their own delusion. The verse displays the worsening of the wicked, over against Timothy's call to continue in the truth (v.14).
14:
"But continue thou in the things which thou hast learned and hast been assured of, knowing of whom thou hast learned them;" The witnesses note the charge to continue in what Timothy has learned and been assured of. On "continue thou in the things which thou hast learned," the grammatical voices note the steadfastness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge over against the worsening deceivers: "continue thou (abide, remain, hold fast) in the things which thou hast learned (been taught)" — Timothy is to stay steadfastly in the sound doctrine he received, not be carried away by the prevailing error.

On "and hast been assured of," the witnesses note the conviction. Barnes and Calvin note the added ground: "and hast been assured of (been convinced of, made fully sure of)" — Timothy had not merely learned these truths but been inwardly persuaded of them; settled conviction, not mere acquaintance, anchors him.

On "knowing of whom thou hast learned them," the puritan Henry notes the trustworthy teachers: "knowing of whom (from whom) thou hast learned them" — from Paul himself, and from his godly mother and grandmother (1:5); the trustworthiness of his teachers is a ground of his confidence; he learned the truth from those whose lives confirmed it. The verse displays the charge to continue in what Timothy has learned.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge — "continue thou (abide, hold fast) in the things which thou hast learned," staying steadfast in sound doctrine over against error (Barnes, Robertson); "and hast been assured of (convinced of)," inwardly persuaded, settled conviction anchoring him (Barnes, Calvin); "knowing of whom thou hast learned them," from Paul and his godly forebears, the teachers' trustworthiness a ground of confidence (Henry). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the continuance: continue thou. The verse displays the content: what thou hast learned. The verse displays the conviction: been assured of. The verse displays the teachers: of whom. The charge displays Timothy holding fast the truth he was taught. The verse displays the continuing: steadfast. The verse displays the assurance: settled. The verse displays the teachers: trustworthy. The charge to continue in what Timothy has learned and been assured of displays the steadying counsel against the worsening error: Timothy is to abide steadfastly in the sound doctrine he was taught and convinced of — confident in it because he knew the trustworthy teachers, Paul and his godly forebears, from whom he learned it. The verse displays the call to continue, grounded in the Scriptures known from childhood (v.15).
15:
"And that from a child thou hast known the holy scriptures, which are able to make thee wise unto salvation through faith which is in Christ Jesus." The witnesses note the holy Scriptures known from childhood, able to make wise unto salvation. On "from a child thou hast known the holy scriptures," the grammatical voices note the early grounding. Barnes and Robertson note Timothy's lifelong knowledge of Scripture: "from a child (from infancy, from his earliest years) thou hast known the holy scriptures (the sacred writings, the Old Testament)" — taught by his godly mother and grandmother (1:5), Timothy had known the Scriptures from his earliest childhood.

On "able to make thee wise unto salvation," the witnesses note the saving power. Barnes and Calvin note the Scriptures' great office: they "are able to make thee wise unto salvation" — to impart the saving wisdom, to lead to and instruct in the way of salvation; the Scriptures are sufficient to make one wise unto eternal life.

On "through faith which is in Christ Jesus," the grammatical-historical note the condition: this saving wisdom is "through faith which is in Christ Jesus" — the Scriptures make wise unto salvation not by bare reading but as they lead to faith in Christ; faith in Christ is the key that unlocks their saving power. The verse displays the holy Scriptures known from childhood, able to make wise unto salvation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Timothy's early grounding — "from a child thou hast known the holy scriptures (the OT)," taught from infancy by his godly forebears (1:5, Barnes, Robertson); the Scriptures "able to make thee wise unto salvation," imparting saving wisdom, sufficient unto eternal life (Barnes, Calvin); "through faith which is in Christ Jesus," the saving power unlocked by faith in Christ, not bare reading (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the saving Scriptures. The verse marks the grounding: from a child. The verse displays the Scriptures: holy. The verse displays the power: wise unto salvation. The verse displays the key: faith in Christ. The Scriptures display the saving wisdom unlocked by faith in Christ. The verse displays the childhood: taught. The verse displays the wisdom: unto salvation. The verse displays the faith: in Christ. The holy Scriptures known from childhood, able to make wise unto salvation, display the foundation of Timothy's faith: from his earliest years he had known the sacred writings, which are able to make him wise unto salvation — through faith in Christ Jesus, the key that unlocks their saving power. The verse displays the saving Scriptures, whose divine inspiration is now affirmed (v.16).
16:
"All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness:" The witnesses note the inspiration and profit of all Scripture — and the translation crux. On the rendering, interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes, with Gill, Chrysostom, and Wesley, follow the received reading (the predicate): "All scripture IS given by inspiration of God (theopneustos — God-breathed), AND IS profitable" — a positive assertion that all Scripture is inspired, then that it is profitable; Chrysostom: "all such is given by inspiration of God; therefore do not doubt." Ellicott and Clarke (with the RV, the Syriac and Vulgate, and many fathers — Origen, Theodoret, Luther) prefer the attributive: "Every scripture inspired by God IS ALSO profitable" — taking "inspired" as describing "scripture" and "profitable" as the predicate, since (they argue) Timothy needed no proof that the OT was inspired. Robertson holds both grammatically possible (the missing article and copula leaving it open), but notes the predicate reading gives "a definite assertion of inspiration."

On "given by inspiration of God," the witnesses note the great word. Barnes and Chrysostom note "theopneustos" (God-breathed) — the Scriptures are breathed out by God, his own utterance, as truly traced to him as the breath of life; the word asserts their divine origin, however the verse be construed.

On "profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness," the grammatical-historical note the fourfold use: Scripture is profitable for "doctrine (teaching truth), reproof (convicting of error and sin), correction (setting right, reformation), and instruction in righteousness (training in right living)" — the complete equipment for teaching and forming the believer. The verse displays the inspiration and profit of all Scripture.

The witnesses agree the substance: Scripture is God-breathed (theopneustos) — his own utterance, of divine origin — and profitable for the fourfold work of doctrine, reproof, correction, and instruction in righteousness, the complete equipment for forming the believer (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom, Ellicott). But the rendering divides them: the predicate reading (AV, Gill, Barnes, Chrysostom, Wesley) asserts "all Scripture IS inspired AND profitable"; the attributive reading (RV, Ellicott, Clarke, with the Syriac, Vulgate, and many fathers) renders "every inspired Scripture is ALSO profitable"; Robertson holds both grammatically possible. The verse displays the inspiration crux. The verse marks the word: theopneustos. The verse displays the debate: inspired-and-profitable, or inspired-therefore-profitable. The verse displays the origin: God-breathed. The verse displays the fourfold use: doctrine, reproof, correction, instruction. The Scripture displays God's own breathed-out word, profitable for every spiritual use. The verse displays the inspiration: of God. The verse displays the profit: fourfold. The verse displays the rendering: disputed. The inspiration and profit of all Scripture display the great charter of the written word and a translation crux: Scripture is God-breathed and profitable for doctrine, reproof, correction, and instruction in righteousness — read either as asserting that all Scripture is inspired and profitable (AV, Barnes, Chrysostom), or that every inspired Scripture is also profitable (RV, Ellicott, Clarke). The verse displays the profitable word, equipping the man of God (v.17).
17:
"That the man of God may be perfect, throughly furnished unto all good works." The witnesses note the Scriptures' aim: to make the man of God complete and equipped. On "that the man of God may be perfect," the grammatical voices note the purpose. Barnes and Robertson note the end of Scripture's profit (v.16): "that the man of God (the believer, especially the minister) may be perfect (artios — complete, fitted, fully developed, lacking nothing)" — the design of the inspired, profitable Scripture is to make the man of God complete in every part.

On "the man of God," the witnesses note the person. Barnes and Calvin note that "the man of God" is the one devoted to God — Timothy as minister, and by extension every servant of God; the Scriptures form and equip him for his whole calling.

On "throughly furnished unto all good works," the grammatical-historical note the equipment: "throughly furnished (exertismenos — fully equipped, completely fitted out, as a workman with all his tools or a ship with all its gear) unto all good works" — Scripture supplies all that is needed for every good work; the man of God lacks nothing for his task. The verse displays the Scriptures' aim: to make the man of God complete and equipped.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose — "that the man of God (the believer, especially the minister) may be perfect (complete, lacking nothing)," Scripture making him complete in every part (Barnes, Robertson); "the man of God" the one devoted to God, Timothy and every servant, formed by the Scriptures (Barnes, Calvin); "throughly furnished (fully equipped) unto all good works," supplied with all needed for every good work, lacking nothing (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the equipped man. The verse marks the aim: the man of God perfect. The verse displays the person: devoted to God. The verse displays the equipment: throughly furnished. The verse displays the scope: all good works. The aim displays Scripture making the man of God complete. The verse displays the perfection: complete. The verse displays the furnishing: full. The verse displays the works: all good. The Scriptures' aim — to make the man of God complete and equipped — displays the sufficiency of the inspired word: its great design is that the man of God may be complete, lacking nothing, and fully furnished for every good work. The verse displays the sufficiency of Scripture, grounding the solemn charge to preach the word (4:1-2).
